subtopic_name: Stability of Closed and Open Loop Control Systems
subtopic_summary: Stability of Closed and Open Loop Control Systems talks about stability characteristics of closed-loop systems, bounded outputs for bounded inputs, unbounded ramp, example with proportional controller showing unstable responses for large controller gain, open-loop stable self-regulating processes returning to steady state after disturbances, characteristic equation from transfer functions, poles and zeros, partial fraction expansion, inverse Laplace transform, system instability from positive real poles or complex poles with positive real parts, stability from negative poles or negative real parts.

General Stability Criterion talks about feedback control system stability requiring all characteristic equation roots negative or with negative real parts, otherwise unstable, complex roots causing oscillatory response occurring in pairs, criterion applying to closed-loop responses from set-point or load changes, characteristic equation depending on controller valve process and measurement transfer functions, denominator determining stability.

Routh Stability Criterion talks about determining number of characteristic equation roots with positive real parts without explicit calculation for stability, limitation to polynomial equations without time delays using Pade approximation for approximate analysis, necessary condition of positive coefficients, Routh array construction with triangular structure, formulas for array elements, stability requiring positive first column elements, example with proportional controller to find stable controller gain values.

Direct Substitution Method talks about imaginary axis dividing stable and unstable systems with zero real part, marginal stability meaning sustained oscillations, two closed-loop complex poles on imaginary axis denoted by j omega and minus j omega, substituting s equals j omega into characteristic equation to find stability limit, maximum controller gain, solving for max controller gain and omega.
